Validated Insight: Flooded paddy RETAINS moisture and microclimate — it does not trap. Flooded anaerobic conditions actively support methanogenic and nitrogen-cycling microbiomes that are unique to rice systems. Full ground sun on dry paddy soils between seasons makes soil sterile and inactivates microbiome — must never leave dry paddy fields bare.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Azolla (MF23 booster), Sesbania (green manure), Duck farming integration
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): MF22 risk is real in dense stands — pair with MF13 in bunded layout. MF3G after harvest = microbiome risk: flag for cover crop requirement.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF3U (upper canopy 2–4m); MF1 partial shade at mid-tier
Validated Insight: Tall maize creates UPPER canopy sun effect (MF3U) at 2–4m height — heat accumulation occurs at canopy level, not ground. Lower zones receive partial shade (MF1). Evapotranspiration at canopy level reduces humidity above — not same as ground drying (MF10). Canopy cooling retains microclimate stability in mid and lower tiers. Deep biopore roots (MF16) persist 2–3 seasons after crop.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cowpea (MF23), Red Gram (MF23+MF27), Pumpkin (MF17+MF18)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Three-tier rule applies: MF3U at top, MF1 at 1–2m, MF3G only if inter-row is unshaded. Intercrop design must specify height band of each companion.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF3G (0–1m); MF3L (1–2m) — both lower bands exposed
Validated Insight: Jowar is a dry microclimate crop — it REQUIREs MF15 (dry atmosphere) and produces MF10 (rapid drying). SUPPRESSES moisture and shade features. Ground-level sun exposure means MF3G is active: risk of soil sterilisation between rows. Alley cropping with legume hedgerows (Red Gram, Sesbania) is critical to protect inter-row soil biology. Deep allelopathic root residues — rotation management needed.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Red Gram (border, MF1 shade + MF23), Horsegram (MF10-tolerant), Sesbania (green manure bund)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): MF3G is active in inter-row — FLAG: soil microbiome protection needed. Pair MF15 (dry) crops with MF21 (trap crop) for pest diversion. Avoid dense spacing — MF12 stagnation risk.

Validated Insight: Extremely drought-tolerant; sandy soil specialist. Similar to Jowar in MF profile but more extreme in dry microclimate dependency (MF15). Inter-row bare soil is the primary soil health risk — ground sun (MF3G) inactivates microbiome rapidly in sandy red soils. Intercrop with cowpea or moth bean on every third row to protect soil life.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cowpea (MF23+MF18), Moth Bean, Cluster Bean (Guar)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Sandy soil alert: MF10+MF15 cascade is faster and more damaging in sandy soils than in clay. Mandatory: inter-row cover crop or mulch to prevent MF3G→soil sterility cascade.

Validated Insight: Ground-hugging crop — dense tillering physically covers soil better than taller crops. Suppresses MF18 (weed suppression) as a co-benefit of dense canopy coverage. SUPPRESSES MF10 through dense crop mat cover. Deep fibrous roots create excellent biopore network that benefits next-season crops. Good erosion buffer on slopes (MF25). Avoid dense shade companions — it actively suppresses MF1 and MF2.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Groundnut (MF3G companion), Horse Gram, Niger
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Ragi's tillering density naturally provides MF18 — no herbicide needed if stand is established. Rotation: follow with Red Gram for MF23 N-fixation benefit.

Validated Insight: Rabi (winter) crop — lower temperature means MF3G does not trigger the soil sterilisation cascade that occurs in summer. Residue mulch post-harvest is critical (MF17) to protect soil entering the hot season. Requires MF11 (wind barrier) protection from dry winter winds in Rayalaseema. Stubble incorporation builds SOM but C:N management needed.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Mustard (border, MF19+MF21), Chickpea (MF23), Lentil
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Rabi season exception: MF3G in winter does NOT carry same microbiome risk as summer. Flag seasonal context in rule engine. Post-harvest MF17 is mandatory for summer transition.

Validated Insight: Short-duration millet (60–90 days) — ideal as intercrop or quick cover. MF14 drought tolerance makes it suitable for rainfed fallows. MF17 topsoil structure benefit from fibrous roots. Activates MF19 through rapid grain formation.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Black Gram (MF23), Sesame (MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): FLAG: Short crop cycle means MF3G exposure between crops — mandatory cover crop or mulch protocol after harvest.

Validated Insight: Little millet thrives in poor soils — activates MF14 in low-fertility red soils. Minimal input requirement makes it a natural-farming-compatible cash crop. MF24 (mineral weathering) supported by fibrous root exudates.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cowpea (MF23), Horse Gram (MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Best suited for marginal red soils in AP uplands — include in dryland crop rotation designs.

Validated Insight: Barnyard millet tolerates waterlogged and poorly drained soils — unique among millets for MF8/MF9 moisture tolerance. Dual use as grain and fodder. MF15 (anaerobic stress MF) activated in heavy soils.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Green Gram (MF23), Cowpea (MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Waterlogging tolerance is key differentiator — include in wet upland or bunded field designs.

Validated Insight: Fastest-maturing millet (60–75 days) — activates MF19 rapidly. Excellent for intercropping and relay systems. MF14 drought tolerance. Shallow fibrous roots contribute to MF17 surface tilth.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Green Gram (MF23), Sesame (MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Very short crop window — useful in relay systems; plan MF3G mitigation post-harvest with quick-cover legume.

Validated Insight: Red Gram is the BACKBONE companion crop of AP natural farming. It simultaneously produces shade (MF1), N-fixation (MF23), P-cycling (MF29), deep biopore roots (MF16), surface mulch (MF17), and beneficial insect habitat (MF19+MF20). The cooling shade it produces RETAINS the microclimate and soil moisture — not traps. BNF rate: 100–200 kg N/ha/yr. Biopore legacy persists 2–3 seasons. Must be in every polyculture system design.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sorghum (MF3G companion), Groundnut, Sesame, Castor (border)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): KEYSTONE companion plant for AP. IF Red Gram PRESENT THEN MF23+MF16+MF27 active → satisfies N-requirement for 1–2 companion cereals without external input. Allelopathy from Castor nearby — maintain >2m spacing.

Validated Insight: Short-duration N-fixer (60–70 days) — ideal relay or short-season intercrop. Ground-zone crop (MF3G) means inter-row soil is EXPOSED if plant spacing is wide — soil microbiome protection needed in gaps. Dense planting at recommended spacing naturally creates ground cover and reduces MF3G exposure. Excellent for paddy-pulse rotation: fixes N immediately after paddy harvest into residue-covered soil.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Paddy (relay), Maize (intercrop), Castor (border row)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Short duration flag: N-benefit is delivered quickly (45–60 days). Time companion crop nutrient demand to peak at 30–45 days after Black Gram establishment.

Validated Insight: Fastest N-fixer for AP conditions (50–60 days). Excellent as green manure — incorporate at flowering stage delivers high-N biomass with low C:N ratio (<15) → rapid mineralisation → N available to next crop within 2–3 weeks. Relay cropping with paddy is the most efficient MF23 delivery system for rice fields. Short canopy means ground protection requires dense planting.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Paddy (relay after harvest), Maize (intercrop), Sugarcane (ratoon intercrop)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Green manure flag: IF incorporated at flowering THEN C:N ~12 → N mineralisation in 2–3 weeks. IF left to mature grain THEN C:N rises to 20+ → slower N release. Time management critical.

Validated Insight: AP's most drought-hardy pulse. SUPPRESSES MF8 (evaporation reduction) — meaning it is adapted to high evaporative conditions and does not itself reduce soil evaporation significantly. This is unusual among pulses. Grows on degraded Alfisols (red soils, pH 5.5–6.5) where other pulses fail. Root exudates improve severely degraded soils. Most important pulse for dryland soil rehabilitation.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Jowar (intercrop), Castor (border), Neem (windbreak)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Soil rehabilitation flag: MF27+MF23+MF16 combination makes Horse Gram uniquely valuable for degraded soil restoration. Sequence: Horse Gram yr1 → Red Gram yr2 → Groundnut yr3 for full soil recovery.

Validated Insight: Dual-purpose N-fixer and living mulch. Dense spreading habit naturally PRODUCES MF18 (weed suppression) — one of the few annual crops that physically occupies all ground space and eliminates weeds without intervention. Fast N-fixation (45 days to significant nodulation). Most versatile intercrop companion for AP — works under maize, with sorghum, in coconut gardens. Heat tolerant — thrives in MF7 zones where other legumes fail.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Maize (classic Three Sisters variant), Sorghum, Coconut garden floor
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Living mulch flag: Cowpea at >80% ground cover suppresses weeds + fixes N + protects soil microbiome simultaneously. Optimal spacing: 30cm within row, 45cm between rows for maximum ground cover.

Validated Insight: SUPPRESSES MF8 — meaning Groundnut itself increases soil evaporation (its open canopy does not reduce radiation receipt). But its pegging action physically loosens topsoil and creates MF16 biopores in the top 10–15cm. This is unique — root channels from pegging action, not taproot depth. Excellent companion with Red Gram: Red Gram provides partial shade reducing MF3G soil sterilisation risk, Groundnut fixes P-cycle (MF27) and loosens soil.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Red Gram (shade + MF23), Castor (border, pest diversion), Bajra
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Pegging biopore flag: MF16 from Groundnut is shallow (10–15cm) vs taproot crops (>60cm). Complementary to Red Gram MF16. Design rule: Groundnut+Red Gram intercrop covers both shallow and deep soil layers.

Validated Insight: Strong allelopathic properties — suppresses germination of many crops and weeds within 2–3m radius. However, allelopathic compounds decompose in 4–6 weeks after incorporation. PRODUCES MF21 (pest diversion) — highly attractive to aphids, pulling them away from main crops. Heliotropic — tracks sun, maximising light interception. Critical to place in border rows for its trap crop function, not in dense intercrop.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Castor (border), Red Gram (distant companion >3m), Maize (same-height border)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Allelopathy management flag: MF26 is active — maintain >2m spacing from legume intercrops (MF23 suppression). Incorporate residues 6+ weeks before planting N-sensitive neighbours.

Validated Insight: Tall, fast border crop with strong allelopathic seed coat — ricin compounds in litter are potent. Deep taproot creates biopore channels (MF16). Excellent trap crop (MF21) for Helicoverpa and Spodoptera — concentrates bollworm on Castor border before it reaches cotton/tomato main crop. Border spacing critical: 1 row Castor per 8–10 rows main crop.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cotton (border trap crop), Tomato (pest diversion), Jowar (intercrop at 1:6 ratio)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Allelopathy flag: Castor litter is strongly allelopathic. Do NOT use as mulch near legumes. Trap crop management: inspect border rows weekly — destroy Castor when pest load exceeds 10/plant.

Validated Insight: Glucosinolate-rich root exudates have biofumigation properties — suppresses soil-borne pathogens and nematodes when incorporated as green manure. Rabi crop — MF3G winter sun does not carry summer microbiome risk. Excellent trap crop (MF21) for aphids. REQUIREs wind barrier (MF11) in AP winter conditions.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Wheat (border), Chickpea (intercrop), Lentil
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Biofumigation flag: incorporate at 30% flowering for maximum glucosinolate content. Wait 2–3 weeks before planting next crop. MF21 aphid trap value highest during flowering.

Validated Insight: Deep taproot creates significant biopore network (MF16). Upper canopy sun (MF3U) means lower zones are in partial shade (MF1). PRODUCES MF22 (pest shelter) — dense bolling-stage canopy creates ideal humidity zone for bollworm and fungal diseases. This is a critical design failure in monocrop cotton. Solution: MF13 (airflow) design + MF19+MF20 (beneficial insect) companions. Castor border for MF21 (trap crop) is essential.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Castor (border, MF21), Red Gram (MF23+MF1 companion), Cowpea (ground cover MF18)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): MF22 risk flag: Cotton monoculture is high MF22 system. Mandatory companions: Castor (pest diversion) + Coriander/Marigold (MF19 beneficial insects) to counter MF22 with MF20+MF21.

Validated Insight: Shade-obligate crop — REQUIRES MF1 (partial shade) to prevent leaf scorch. Cannot grow under full ground sun (MF3G). Allelopathic rhizome exudates (MF26) — curcumin derivatives inhibit soil pathogens AND neighbouring root growth. Best grown under Banana (MF1 provider) or Coconut (MF4-PAR). Produces dense leaf mulch (MF17) that feeds surface microbiome. Cooling under companion canopy RETAINS the microclimate needed for rhizome development.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Banana (MF1+MF6 shade provider), Coconut garden, Areca + Black Pepper system
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): MF1 requirement is non-negotiable: Turmeric in full sun = yield failure. Design rule: MUST have canopy tree (MF1 producer) within 2m. MF26 allelopathy — do not plant adjacent to legumes without 1m buffer.

Validated Insight: Even more shade-demanding than Turmeric — requires 50–70% canopy cover (MF1 producing companion). High organic matter demand — produces dense mulch (MF17) and relies on active rhizosphere microbiome (MF27). REQUIRES MF14 (high humidity) — canopy cooling RETAINS the humid microclimate essential for rhizome bulking. Cooling under canopy is not a trap — it is the functional environment for ginger physiology.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Areca Nut (classic companion), Banana, Coconut garden floor
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Dual REQ flag: Ginger REQUIRES both MF1 (shade) AND MF14 (humidity) simultaneously. Any system design must provide both. Areca+Banana combination is the AP standard for satisfying both requirements.

Validated Insight: Capsaicin in fruit deters some chewing insects — minor MF21 function. REQUIREs MF13 (airflow) — one of the most airflow-dependent vegetables. Dense planting without airflow = Anthracnose and Phytophthora devastation. Companion with Onion (sulfur exudates deter thrips) and Marigold (MF19+MF20) is classical AP practice.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Onion (pest deterrence), Marigold (MF19), Basil (aromatic repellent), Coriander (MF19)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Airflow is NON-NEGOTIABLE for Chilli. Design rule: maximum 3 rows chilli before airflow break row (Marigold, Coriander, or gap). MF13 must be maintained throughout crop cycle.

Validated Insight: Dense perennial clump creates strong wind barrier (MF11) and shade (MF1+MF2). RETAINS microclimate in shelter zone — not traps. Deep root system (MF16) creates biopore channels and erosion control (MF25). Living fence use: plant on field bund as 1.5–2m height windbreak. One row of Napier can protect 10–15m of leeward field from desiccating winds. Pollard every 60 days — biomass is excellent MF17 mulch material.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sesbania (bund companion, MF23), Gliricidia (mixed hedge, MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Living fence design: Napier+Gliricidia combination creates wind protection (MF11) + N-fixation (MF23) in one bund structure. Pollarding schedule: every 45–60 days for biomass + maintained height.

Validated Insight: Perennial N-fixing fodder hedge for AP dryland systems. Produces MF1 (partial shade) as a living hedge — protects adjacent crop from MF3G solar sterilisation. BNF rate: 80–120 kg N/ha/yr. Extremely drought tolerant after establishment. Alley crop configuration: 2 rows Desmanthus per 6 rows cereal = 30% N-input replacement.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sorghum (alley crop), Jowar, Bajra (dryland cereals)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Alley crop flag: Desmanthus hedge provides MF1 shade + MF23 N + MF16 biopores simultaneously. Design rule: 2 hedge rows per 6 crop rows. Pollard to 50cm at crop establishment to prevent shade competition.

Validated Insight: Extremely low-input ground cover N-fixer. Colonises degraded red soils where nothing else establishes. Acts as LIVING MULCH (MF17) protecting soil microbiome from MF3G solar sterilisation. Critical for AP's degraded Alfisols — year-round soil cover in dryland systems. BNF rate: 40–80 kg N/ha/yr. Seed naturally into inter-rows of perennial crops.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cashew (under-canopy ground cover), Mango orchard floor, Teak plantation
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Degraded soil pioneer flag: Stylosanthes is the MF3G-to-MF17 transition crop. IF soil is bare degraded Alfisol THEN establish Stylosanthes first for 1 season THEN introduce higher-value polyculture.

Validated Insight: Cool-season shade-tolerant GLV. In AP summer, REQUIREs MF1 partial shade to prevent bolting. Shade RETAINS cooler microclimate essential for leaf quality. Dense leaf canopy provides MF18 weed suppression at ground level. Companion with taller crops that produce MF1.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (MF1 provider), Drumstick (MF1 hedge), Moringa border
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Seasonal flag: Rabi/winter only in AP lowlands without shade. Summer production requires MF1 companion system. Shade cooling RETAINS quality, not traps — leaf expansion better in diffused light.

Validated Insight: Heat-tolerant GLV; thrives in MF7 zones where cool-season crops fail. Ground cover with dense leaf production naturally provides MF18. Rapid growth — 3–4 cuts possible per season. Excellent companion under taller crops for vertical space utilisation.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (ground companion), Brinjal, Chilli
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Heat tolerance asset: Amaranth can fill MF7 zones where other GLVs fail. Use as inter-row ground cover in Kharif vegetable systems.

Validated Insight: N-fixing GLV — dual role as food and soil builder. Allelopathic to some soil fungi (antifungal compounds in root exudates). Mulch after harvest provides MF17. Short cycle (45 days) allows quick N-fixation credit to next crop.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Onion (companion), Potato (intercrop), Coriander (mixed GLV bed)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): N-fixation GLV flag: BNF rate modest (20–40 kg N/ha/yr) but combined with mulch contribution makes it a high-value rotation crop. Antifungal exudates benefit solanaceous crops in rotation.

Validated Insight: One of the most valuable GLV/companion trees in AP. Open feathery canopy provides MF4-STR (beam scatter) and MF1 (partial shade) without blocking all light. Deep taproot (MF16) creates excellent biopore network. Leaf litter is high-N mulch (MF17; C:N ~15) — decomposes rapidly. SUPPRESSES MF3G — shade from Moringa prevents ground solar sterilisation. Grows fast (first harvest 8 months, perennial thereafter).
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (shade companion), Chilli, Vegetables (hedge row), Paddy bund
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Keystone GLV tree flag: Moringa as a hedgerow satisfies MF1+MF17+MF16+MF23-adjacent simultaneously. Design: plant 3–5m apart on field bund. Prune to 1.5m height for MF1 maintenance without MF2 overcast.

Validated Insight: Trellis canopy RETAINS cool moist microclimate below — not traps. Leaf canopy on trellis creates MF1 partial shade that protects companion crops at ground level. Traditional AP intercrop: Bitter Gourd on trellis over ground-level vegetables. High medicinal and nutraceutical value — produces complex secondary metabolites requiring stable cool microclimate.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (MF19 companion below trellis), Coriander, Marigold (MF20)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Trellis microclimate design: Bitter Gourd trellis at 1.8m creates MF1 partial shade zone below — use for spinach, coriander, or basil ground layer. Airflow through trellis must be maintained — avoid MF12 stagnation.

Validated Insight: Traditional AP household crop on trellis or pergola. Large leaves create effective MF1 shade zone below. High transpiration rate (MF6 co-benefit under dense planting). Good pollinator attractor (MF19) during long flowering period.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Bitter Gourd (mixed trellis), Ridge Gourd, Basil (below trellis)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Mixed trellis design: Snake Gourd + Ridge Gourd + Bitter Gourd on same trellis = continuous harvest + combined MF1 shade effect throughout season.

Validated Insight: Large leaves create significant MF1 shade whether sprawling on ground or on trellis. Sprawling habit = excellent ground cover (MF18 weed suppression, MF8 evaporation reduction). Leaf litter after harvest provides MF17. High water content of fruit concentrates moisture in plant — evapotranspiration contributes to MF14 in immediate microzone.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cowpea (ground companion MF23), Maize (trellis support), Basil
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Ground cover mode vs trellis mode have different MF profiles. Specify in design: Ground mode → MF18 active. Trellis mode → MF1 active below. Both produce MF17 via leaf fall.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF1 (partial shade from large leaves) + MF3G (sprawling ground)
Validated Insight: Classic Three Sisters companion (Maize-Bean-Pumpkin). Large leaves create BOTH ground shade (suppresses MF3G sterilisation) AND MF18 weed suppression. Sprawling habit covers maximum ground area. Leaf mulch (MF17) after season end is very high biomass. Ideal for covering inter-row space in any tall crop system.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Maize + Cowpea (Three Sisters), Sugarcane inter-row
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Three Sisters flag: Maize (vertical) + Cowpea (MF23) + Pumpkin (MF18 ground cover) is the classic multi-MF polyculture. Combined: MF1+MF23+MF18+MF17+MF16 in one planting design.

Validated Insight: Ground-sprawling MF3G crop that paradoxically SUPPRESSES MF3G sterilisation in its own row through leaf cover. Requires MF10 (dry microclimate) for fruit set — wet roots cause fungal collapse. Allelopathic to most weeds (natural weed suppressor in row). Sandy red soils (Andhra coastal plains) ideal. High irrigation demand but leaf canopy reduces evaporation between plants.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sesame (border row, compatible dry microclimate), Maize (windbreak border)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Dry-wet paradox: Watermelon REQUIRES well-drained dry soil (MF10) but needs regular irrigation. The plant manages its own microclimate — leaf cover reduces soil evaporation but fruit zone must drain freely.

Validated Insight: Similar MF profile to Watermelon — dry microclimate essential. Highly aromatic flowers are excellent MF19 (pollinator attractor). Companion with Basil improves pollinator visits and may reduce pest pressure. Sandy loam soils of Krishna, Godavari coastal AP are ideal.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (aromatic companion, MF19 enhancement), Marigold
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Pollinator dependency flag: Muskmelon has poor fruit set without adequate MF19 (pollinator zone). Design: 1 Marigold/Basil plant per 5 Muskmelon plants as pollinator corridor.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF1 (minor shade from foliage) + MF3G requirement
Validated Insight: REQUIRES MF13 (airflow) — most airflow-sensitive vegetable in AP. Dense planting without airflow = Early Blight and Late Blight devastation. REQUIREs MF3G (full ground sun) for fruiting but benefits from minor MF1 (partial leaf shade) in peak summer. ALLELOPATHIC to fennel — never plant together. Companion with Basil (aromatic pest deterrent) and Marigold (MF19+MF20) is the most validated AP combination.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (essential aromatic companion), Marigold (MF19+MF20), Onion (thrips deterrence), Coriander
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Airflow NON-NEGOTIABLE: Minimum 60cm between rows for MF13. Design rule: every 3rd row = Marigold or Basil break row for MF13 maintenance + MF19 benefit.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF3G + minor MF1 from own canopy
Validated Insight: Trap crop function for Shoot and Fruit Borer (Leucinodes orbonalis) — Brinjal planted as border row around tomato attracts SFB preferentially (MF21 function). However Brinjal requires active management: inspect weekly, cut and destroy infested shoots. Companion with Coriander provides MF19+MF20 benefit.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Coriander (MF19 companion), Marigold, Onion (border)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Trap crop flag: Brinjal as border row around Tomato provides MF21 function for SFB. Requires weekly scouting and shoot removal. Do not allow SFB to build on trap crop — manage within 2-week window.

Validated Insight: Capsaicin in leaf trichomes has minor pest deterrent effect (MF21 function). Like Tomato, REQUIRES MF13 (airflow) — Anthracnose and Colletotrichum are humidity-driven. Onion companion with sulfur exudates is the most validated AP pest management companion. Marigold (MF19) for thrips parasitoid attraction.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Onion (sulfur compounds deter thrips), Marigold (MF19), Coriander (MF19), Basil
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Airflow + humidity management: Chilli is the crop most sensitive to MF14 (high humidity) build-up. Design: never plant in MF12 (stagnant air) zones. Wind barrier (MF11) must not create full stagnation — use permeable barrier.

Validated Insight: Tuber expansion mechanically loosens soil — creates shallow MF16 biopores (10–20cm). SUPPRESSES MF14 — must have dry air for Late Blight prevention. Companion with Horseradish is classical allelopathic companion (Horseradish deters Colorado potato beetle through glucosinolate exudates). AVOID near Tomato — shared solanaceous diseases.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Horseradish (border, allelopathic pest deterrence), Beans (N-fixation), Coriander (MF19)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Disease flag: Potato and Tomato share Late Blight (Phytophthora infestans) — NEVER intercrop. Spatial rule: >10m separation or physical barrier between Potato and Tomato fields.

Validated Insight: Tall upright crop with exposed inter-row ground — MF3G soil sterilisation risk in row middles. Strong pollinator attractor (MF19) — large flowers. Companion with Basil provides aromatic pest deterrence. Ground cover between Okra rows is essential — Coriander or Fenugreek as inter-row companion protects soil microbiome.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (pest deterrence), Coriander (MF19 + ground cover), Marigold
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Ground protection flag: Okra inter-row ground is exposed to MF3G. Mandatory: inter-row companion plant (Coriander, Fenugreek, or Basil) for microbiome protection.

Validated Insight: Sulfur-containing volatile compounds (allicin, diallyl disulfide) deter many pest insects — Onion is one of the most effective aromatic companion plants in AP vegetable systems. Very low canopy means ground is essentially fully exposed (MF3G active throughout) — inter-row mulch is critical. Companion with Carrot is classical: Onion deters carrot fly, Carrot deters onion fly.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (pest deterrence), Chilli (thrips reduction), Carrot (mutual deterrence), Beets
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Aromatic companion value: Onion sulfur compounds act as MF21-equivalent aromatic pest diversion within 1m radius. Use in border rows around solanaceous crops for thrips management.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): REQ: MF1 (partial shade essential for establishment)
Validated Insight: Large leaf canopy RETAINS cool moist microclimate once established — not traps. Requires partial shade (MF1) for establishment but tolerates more sun when mature. Deep corm acts as water reservoir in dry conditions. Dense leaf mulch production (MF17) feeds surface microbiome. Classic companion crop under Banana or Coconut in AP homestead gardens.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Banana (MF1+MF6 shade provider), Coconut garden floor, Areca garden
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Shade requirement transition: Young Elephant Yam REQUIRES MF1 strictly. Mature plant tolerates moderate sun. Design: establish under existing MF1 tree canopy; once established can expand into partial MF3G zones.

Validated Insight: AP's most important agroforestry canopy tree. Dense canopy REFLECTS NIR (MF4-NIR) — removes 40–50% of solar heat load from understory WITHOUT reducing PAR availability. Cooling under Mango canopy RETAINS the microclimate that supports understory crop biology. Deep taproot creates extensive biopore network. Leaf litter (C:N ~30) builds SOM slowly but steadily. Upper canopy sun (MF3U) drives canopy ET — not ground drying.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Curry Leaf (understory), Drumstick (sub-canopy), Turmeric (ground layer), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Keystone agroforestry flag: Mango as canopy anchor satisfies MF1+MF4-NIR+MF6+MF17 simultaneously. Design: 1 Mango per 100m² provides adequate MF1+MF4 coverage for 2–3 understory crop rows.

Validated Insight: Strongly aromatic flowers create intense MF19 (pollinator attraction) during flowering. Allelopathic fallen leaf litter — Citrus litter leachates inhibit germination of many ground-level companions. Companion with Basil improves pollinator visits. REQUIREs MF13 (airflow) for disease prevention (citrus canker, gummosis). SUPPRESSES MF3G — canopy prevents ground sterilisation.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (MF19 enhancement), Drumstick (sub-canopy companion), Cowpea (ground)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Litter allelopathy flag: Citrus fallen leaves are allelopathic. Rake fallen leaves from under tree for composting — do not leave as in-situ mulch if establishing new understorey plants.

Validated Insight: Hardy canopy provider — strong MF1 zone below. Allelopathic leaf litter documented — guava leachates suppress some crop germination. Dense canopy SUPPRESSES MF3G effectively. High medicinal value. Companion with Banana (microclimate contrast — Guava drier, Banana wetter) not recommended — incompatible MF profiles. Best as standalone shade tree in vegetable garden.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cowpea (ground cover below), Drumstick (companion sub-canopy), Marigold (border MF19)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Microclimate note: Guava creates MF1 zone with moderate humidity — suitable for shade-tolerant vegetables and spices below. Litter allelopathy: allow 2–3 week breakdown before establishing seedlings nearby.

Validated Insight: Fast pioneer canopy tree — provides MF3L partial shade quickly (fruiting in 9–12 months). REQUIRES wind protection (MF11) — extremely susceptible to wind damage. Root system very sensitive to waterlogging. Companion with Basil and Marigold for MF19+MF20 benefit.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (MF19+MF21 companion), Marigold, Cowpea (ground MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Wind protection flag: Papaya MUST have MF11 windbreak on prevailing wind side. Use Napier Grass or Casuarina as windbreak. Plant 1m from windbreak on leeward side.

Validated Insight: Dense evergreen canopy creates strong MF4-FR (far-red enrichment zone) below — morphological shade signal for understory plants. Large fruits sequester significant carbon. Leaf litter (MF17) is high volume and medium C:N. RETAINS cool moist microclimate below — ideal for root crops and rhizome crops that require stable deep-shade conditions. AP's most important native canopy tree for multi-tier systems.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Turmeric (deep shade ground layer), Ginger, Black Pepper (climber on trunk), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Far-red signal flag: MF4-FR zone below Jackfruit induces shade morphology in understory crops — choose far-red ADAPTED crops (Ginger, Turmeric, Cardamom) not shade-AVOIDING crops (cereals, most vegetables).

Validated Insight: AP's most productive multi-use canopy tree — food, timber, fodder, shade in one. Dense evergreen canopy creates strong MF4-FR zone below. Large leaf litter (MF17) is high volume mulch. RETAINS cool moist microclimate below — ideal for shade-dependent spice crops. Deep root system creates excellent MF16 biopore network throughout lifetime of tree.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Turmeric (ground layer MF4-FR adapted), Ginger, Black Pepper (trunk climber), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Multi-function canopy: Jackfruit satisfies MF1+MF2+MF4-FR+MF6+MF17+MF25 simultaneously. Best canopy anchor for shade-spice systems. Companion crops must be FAR-RED ADAPTED — select from: Ginger, Turmeric, Cardamom, Cocoa.

Validated Insight: Dryland fruit tree — adapted to AP's Deccan Plateau climate. Dense canopy provides MF1 zone. Unusual pollination biology — flowers emit strong yeast-like aroma attracting specific beetles (not bees). Companion with Drumstick and Ber in dryland food forest designs.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Drumstick (companion), Ber (dryland companion), Cowpea (ground MF23)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Pollinator specificity: Custard Apple pollination depends on specific beetles — aromatic companion plants (Basil, Marigold) less useful than in bee-pollinated crops. Design: ensure no broad-spectrum insecticides used within 50m.

Validated Insight: Drought-tolerant commercial fruit. Dense root system creates significant MF16 biopore network. SUPPRESSES MF14 — dry microclimate preferred, high humidity causes Cercospora leaf spot and fruit cracking. Companion with Onion deters some insect pests through sulfur volatiles.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Onion (pest deterrence), Coriander (MF19 border), Cowpea (ground MF23 + MF18)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Humidity sensitivity: Pomegranate in MF14 zone = fruit cracking + disease. Design rule: plant in windward-facing position for maximum MF13 airflow. Pair with MF13 companions (open structure border plants).

Validated Insight: Tannin-rich leaf litter inhibits some pathogens (antifungal allelopathy) while improving soil microbiome (MF27). One of the few trees with documented simultaneous allelopathic + microbiome-enhancing properties. Companion with Basil improves pollinator visits. Excellent dryland food forest tree.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Basil (MF19), Drumstick (companion sub-canopy), Cowpea (ground)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Dual soil biology flag: Amla litter — tannins suppress harmful soil fungi (benefit) AND stimulate beneficial decomposer bacteria (benefit). Unique positive allelopathy profile — use as deliberate companion for soil health.

Validated Insight: Classic AP multi-tier plantation system: Areca creates partial shade (MF1) while requiring upper canopy sun (MF3U) from Coconut or sky above. The inter-areca microclimate RETAINS humidity and cooling — ideal for Black Pepper (climber on trunk) and Ginger/Turmeric at ground level. Traditional Areca gardens in East Godavari and West Godavari are among the most complex natural farming polycultures in AP.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Black Pepper (climber on trunk), Ginger/Turmeric (ground), Banana (inter-areca), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Multi-tier plantation flag: Areca+Black Pepper+Ginger is AP's classic three-level spice system. All three MF profiles are mutually satisfying: Areca PROD(MF1+MF14) → Pepper REQ(MF1+MF14) → Ginger REQ(MF1+MF6+MF14). No external MF input needed.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): REQ: MF1+MF4-PAR (strict shade requirement; PAR-filtered light only)
Validated Insight: Strictest shade requirement in AP commercial crops — REQUIRES MF4-PAR (PAR-filtered diffused light). Cannot establish under dense MF2 (too dark) or full MF3G (sun-damaged). The specific light quality — diffused PAR with NIR removed — is exactly the forest understory environment. Canopy RETAINS the humid microclimate essential for pod development. Classic shade system: Coconut or Jackfruit above → Banana sub-canopy → Cocoa at ground level.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Coconut (canopy, MF4-PAR provider), Jackfruit (dense shade), Banana (sub-canopy), Ginger
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): MF4-PAR requirement is precise: NOT dense shade (MF2), NOT full sun (MF3G) — specifically DIFFUSED PAR at 30–50% open sky equivalent. Coconut palm canopy is the ideal MF4-PAR provider in AP conditions.

Validated Insight: Classic shade-grown crop requiring MF4-STR (beam scatter) — not just any shade, but scattered diffuse light. Silver Oak (Grevillea) is the AP standard shade companion — its pinnate leaves create ideal scatter profile. Cooling under shade canopy RETAINS the humid microclimate essential for cherry development and bean quality. Research confirms: shade-grown coffee has higher cup quality AND lower disease pressure.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Silver Oak/Grevillea (MF4-STR shade provider), Banana (sub-canopy), Cardamom (companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Light quality flag: Coffee requires MF4-STR specifically — Grevillea's open pinnate canopy creates ideal beam scatter. Solid shade trees (Jackfruit, Mango at close spacing) produce MF2 which is too dark. Design: 8×8m Grevillea spacing for coffee.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): REQ: MF1+MF11+MF14 (shade + protection + humidity all required)
Validated Insight: Most microclimate-sensitive crop in AP. Requires ALL THREE of: shade (MF1), wind protection (MF11), AND high humidity (MF14) simultaneously. Traditional Betel Leaf gardens (Tambula gardens) in AP's East Godavari and Krishna districts are elaborate microclimate engineering systems — bamboo shade structures, irrigation misting, and dense windbreaks all used together.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Areca (shade + support system), Bamboo windbreak (MF11), Banana (humidity + shade)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Triple REQ flag: Betel Leaf is the most demanding microclimate crop. System design MUST satisfy MF1+MF11+MF14 simultaneously — failure of any one = crop failure. Traditional AP Tambula gardens are the gold standard reference design.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): REQ: MF1+MF4-STR (scattered diffuse light; deep shade)
Validated Insight: Deep shade crop native to Western Ghats forest understory — the highest MF4 requirement of any AP crop. Requires beam scatter (MF4-STR) plus PAR filtration (MF4-PAR) simultaneously. Silver Oak in AP's Araku Valley is the traditional companion. Cooling under canopy RETAINS the stable humid microclimate that Cardamom requires for capsule development — this is a classic case of RETAINING, not trapping.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Silver Oak (Araku Valley standard shade), Banana (sub-canopy humidity), Coffee (companion crop)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Araku Valley flag: Cardamom grown in Araku Valley AP under Silver Oak is a documented success system. MF4-STR requirement met by Silver Oak's open pinnate canopy. Humidity retained (not trapped) under multi-tier system.

Validated Insight: Dryland medicinal — thrives under AP's Rayalaseema dry microclimate conditions. SUPPRESSES MF2 and MF14 — high humidity causes root rot. Deep taproot creates biopore network (MF16) and accesses deep moisture in Alfisols. Aromatic flowers attract pollinators (MF19). REQUIREs wind exposure and low humidity — plant in open well-drained positions.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Neem (windbreak companion), Drumstick (distant shade — >3m), Sesame (companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Dry microclimate design: Ashwagandha must NOT be placed in MF14 (high humidity) zones. Plant on ridges or slopes with good drainage. Compatible with MF15 (dry microatmosphere) producing companions.

Validated Insight: The most versatile aromatic companion plant in AP. Simultaneously: MF19 (pollinator attractor), MF20 (beneficial insect habitat), MF21 (aromatic pest repellent). Eugenol and methyl chavicol volatile compounds deter aphids, whitefly, mites within 1m radius. REQUIREs MF13 (airflow) — does not tolerate stagnant air. Sacred plant in every AP homestead — dual spiritual and functional companion role.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (essential companion), Chilli, Okra, ALL vegetables
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Universal companion flag: Tulsi provides MF19+MF20+MF21 simultaneously — plant at 3m intervals in any vegetable garden for comprehensive biotic protection. Most cost-effective natural farming companion plant in AP.

Validated Insight: Azadirachtin in leaves and seeds is a powerful pest deterrent (MF21/MF26 function). Deep taproot creates biopore network. Open canopy provides MF1 without creating dense MF2 — ideal windbreak/shade tree. Leaf litter is allelopathic but breaks down in 3–4 weeks. Mulch value: Neem litter suppresses soil pathogens AND feeds microbiome simultaneously. Classic AP village tree — used as windbreak, shade, biopesticide source.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): All AP crops benefit from Neem windbreak, Drumstick (companion hedgerow), Moringa
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Biopesticide design: Neem as border/windbreak (MF11) provides MF21 function passively through volatile emissions AND provides MF1 shade + MF16 biopores. Triple function in one tree. Azadirachtin concentration highest in seeds — harvest for neem oil preparation.

Validated Insight: The most important soil and water conservation grass for AP slopes. Roots reach 3–4m depth — unmatched MF16 biopore formation AND MF25 erosion resistance. Surface grass clump provides MF18 (weed suppression in bund zone). Aromatic — minor MF21 function. Bund planting on contour: 0.5m interval rows on >3% slopes is the AP Watershed standard.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Plant on field bunds with any crop system, Stylosanthes (ground cover companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Erosion control priority: Vetiver bund on contour is MANDATORY for AP slopes >3% under natural farming. Root depth of 3–4m means MF16 biopore network operates far below root zone of annual crops — water infiltration benefit extends deep into soil profile.

Validated Insight: Marigold is the most powerful MF23 (biological pest suppressor) activator in horticulture — root exudates suppress root-knot nematodes and soil pathogens. Strong MF12 (aromatic volatile) producer. AP Natural Farming strongly recommends marigold border planting with all vegetable crops.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Tomato (nematode suppression), Brinjal (MF23 border), Chilli (MF22 buffer)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): AP NF flagship companion crop — always recommend as border plant. MF23 activation is scientifically validated for nematode suppression in vegetable plots.

Validated Insight: Chrysanthemum is a commercial cut-flower crop of high value in AP. Strong MF12 (volatile aromatic) producer — pyrethrins in foliage act as natural pesticide. MF19 activation through abundant flower biomass. Requires well-drained loam and cool nights for flowering.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Marigold (MF23 border), Coriander (MF12 complement)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): High-value commercial flower — flag MF13 (soil compaction risk) in pot/greenhouse systems. Incorporate natural farming sprays (JMS, plant extracts) for pest management.

Validated Insight: Rose is a perennial flowering shrub with high MF27 (perennial root zone rejuvenation) potential. MF12 (aromatic volatile) production is economically significant. Deep root system builds MF17 and MF24. Black spot and aphid pressure = high MF22 risk — key site for natural farming spray protocols.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Garlic (MF23 companion), Marigold (MF23 border), Lavender (MF12 synergy)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Perennial woody shrub — MF27 rule applies. Flag MF22 (fungal/pest pressure) as design risk and specify NF spray schedule. Avoid chemical fungicide use.

Validated Insight: Crossandra (Kanakambaram) is a major commercial flower crop of Andhra Pradesh — widely grown for garland and puja use. Strong MF12 and MF17 activator due to dense shallow roots. Shade-tolerant — can be intercropped under coconut and areca nut plantations.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Coconut (shade intercrop), Areca nut (shade intercrop), Marigold (MF23 border)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): AP-specific commercial flower — note shade intercropping potential under plantation crops (coconut, areca). FLAG: High humidity under shade increases MF22 (fungal) risk — monitor.

Validated Insight: Tuberose is a bulbous flower crop — highly fragrant MF12 producer. Bulb-to-bulb propagation builds MF17 (surface soil structure) through annual replanting cycles. Widely grown in AP for perfumery and garlands. MF15 (anaerobic stress) risk in waterlogged conditions — requires well-drained beds.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Marigold (MF23 border), Chrysanthemum (companion bed)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Bulb crop — MF13 compaction risk in clay soils. Raised bed design recommended. FLAG: Do not replant continuously in same soil without rotation — nematode buildup risk.

Validated Insight: Gaillardia is a drought-tolerant annual flower ideal for MF14 (dry-stress resilience) farming systems. Strong pollinator attractant — boosts MF23 (beneficial insect population). Low water requirement and sandy soil tolerance make it AP dryland compatible.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Marigold (MF23 synergy), Sesame (dryland companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Good dryland border flower for natural farming plots — include in low-rainfall farm designs as pollinator attractor.

Validated Insight: Celosia is a heat-tolerant annual with excellent MF14 response under dry conditions. Edible leaves (used as green leafy vegetable in tribal areas). MF17 surface root contribution. Attracts beneficial insects — MF23 support. Dual use as ornamental and edible crop.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Marigold (MF23 border), Amaranth (GLV companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Dual-use ornamental/edible — flag in intercropping systems as companion with vegetables. MF14 tolerance makes it suitable for summer flower cultivation.

Validated Insight: China Aster is a commercially important cut-flower crop in AP. MF12 (volatile aromatic) mild producer. Sensitive to Fusarium wilt — MF22 (fungal pathogen) risk is high. AP NF protocols recommend soil enrichment with Jeevamrutha and trichoderma for wilt suppression. Annual crop with MF19 (seed biomass) activation.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Marigold (MF23 nematode suppression), Coriander (MF12 companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): FLAG: Fusarium wilt risk = critical MF22 design point. Mandatory soil treatment with Jeevamrutha before planting. Crop rotation with legume essential.

Validated Insight: Lotus is an aquatic sacred flower of AP — thrives in still water bodies, ponds, and temple tanks. Unique MF8/MF9/MF15 (wet, aquatic) soil-water microenvironment activator. Rhizome is edible. MF25 (bio-mineral cycling) activated through leaf litter decomposition in pond sediment. Strong MF23 (beneficial microbiome) in pond ecosystem.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Azolla (MF23 aquatic companion), Water Hyacinth (MF25 complement — controlled)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Aquatic/amphibious crop — unique water MF rules apply. FLAG: Do not apply in flowing water systems. Integrate with fish farming for MF23 pond ecosystem design.

Validated Insight: Dense deciduous canopy creates strong MF4-FR (far-red enrichment) zone. Leaf litter allelopathy documented — Teak litter inhibits germination of some species. Large deciduous leaves produce high-volume MF17 mulch in dry season — this is a seasonal pulse of biomass input. Deep root system creates significant MF16 biopore network. Use with caution as sole canopy — mono-teak plantation is a poor natural farming system due to allelopathic litter.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sesbania (MF23 companion in young plantation), Stylosanthes (ground cover), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Allelopathy management: Teak litter pulse (Nov–Jan) provides biomass but may suppress understorey. Rake litter for compost rather than in-situ mulch if establishing new understorey. Multi-species teak+companion planting far superior to mono-teak.

Validated Insight: Ideal multi-purpose agroforestry canopy tree for AP. Unlike Teak, Neem allelopathy breaks down in 3–4 weeks — manageable in a dynamic polyculture. Biopesticide function (MF26/MF21) passive through volatile emissions. Open canopy provides MF1 without MF2 overcast. Deep root (MF16) creates extensive biopore network. Every AP natural farm should have Neem as windbreak/shade tree.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Red Gram (MF23 companion), Drumstick (sub-canopy), Cowpea (ground)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Ideal shade tree profile: Neem produces MF1+MF6+MF16+MF17+MF19+MF21 simultaneously. RETAINS cool microclimate below through canopy cooling. Most cost-effective multi-function canopy tree for AP dryland systems.

Validated Insight: N-fixing canopy tree (Frankia and rhizobia nodules in roots). Produces MF23 at tree scale — significant N contribution to understory. Good honey tree (MF19 function). Seed oil is AP's traditional biopesticide and biodiesel source. Deep taproot creates major MF16 biopore network. Excellent shade tree for agroforestry bunds.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Drumstick (sub-canopy companion), Red Gram (ground N-fixer), Cowpea
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): N-fixing tree flag: Pongamia BNF rate estimated 40–80 kg N/ha/yr at mature tree — significant contribution to adjacent crop N-budget. Design: 1 Pongamia per 200m² provides adequate N-credit to 1–2 companion rows.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF1+MF2 (very dense canopy) + MF4-NIR (strong NIR reflector)
Validated Insight: Long-lived keystone village tree — 100+ year lifespan. STRONGLY ALLELOPATHIC: suppresses N-fixers (MF23) within 5m radius through tartaric acid leachates. The dense canopy RETAINS cool moist microclimate — not traps — but the allelopathic environment severely limits companion planting options. Place only allelopathy-tolerant crops nearby. Fruit/bark have high medicinal and culinary value.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Stylosanthes (allelopathy-tolerant ground cover), Curry Leaf (tolerant sub-canopy)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): ALLELOPATHY ALERT (repeated): Tamarind = 5m exclusion zone for all legumes and N-fixers. Only place allelopathy-TOLERANT crops: Stylosanthes (ground), Curry Leaf (sub-canopy), some grasses. Long-term design: Tamarind as solitary keystone tree with no companion legumes.

Validated Insight: N-fixing timber tree (Rhizobium nodules in roots). Open pinnate canopy creates dappled shade similar to Neem — MF1 without MF2 overcast. Deep root system creates major MF16 biopore legacy — roots penetrate 4–6m in AP's deep Alfisols. Important bund tree for AP agri-horticulture systems.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Drumstick (sub-canopy), Red Gram (companion annual MF23), Cowpea (ground)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Timber+N-fix+biopore flag: Indian Rosewood provides timber value + MF23 (N-fixation) + MF16 (deep biopores) simultaneously. Ideal for long-term agroforestry investment on bunds and farm boundaries.

Validated Insight: N-fixing companion tree par excellence. BNF rate: 100–150 kg N/ha/yr at mature pollarded tree. Classic alley crop tree: planted in rows 2–4m wide alleys, pollarded at crop planting for green manure (MF17). Leaf N content: 3.5–4.5% (comparable to urea at equivalent application). AP's most important N-fixing tree for transitioning farmers.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): All cereals (alley crop), Red Gram (companion), Coconut (inter-canopy companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Alley crop design: Gliricidia at 5m row spacing in 2.5m alleys provides 40–60 kg N/ha/yr to companion cereal rows — equivalent to 90–130 kg urea/ha. Pollard at 0.5m height at crop sowing, and again at mid-season.

Key Light MF (Spectral Zone): MF1+MF3L (open pinnate canopy; ideal beam scatter)
Validated Insight: THE standard shade companion for Coffee and Cardamom in AP's Araku Valley. Pinnate leaf structure creates ideal MF4-STR (beam scatter) — dappled light without solid shade. Fast growing — provides shade in 2–3 years. Deep root system (MF16) does not compete significantly with shallow-rooted coffee. Low allelopathy — one of the few AP trees compatible with all understorey crops.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Coffee (standard companion), Cardamom (Araku system), Black Pepper, Turmeric
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Coffee+Cardamom shade standard: Silver Oak at 8×8m spacing = optimal MF4-STR for Arabica coffee and Cardamom in Araku Valley. No allelopathy concerns — compatible with all understorey companion crops.

Validated Insight: Dense clump creates strong wind barrier (MF11) AND partial to dense shade (MF1–MF2). Rapid biomass producer — MF17 from fallen culms and leaves is among the highest per-hectare biomass in AP. Silica cycling from bamboo litter strengthens neighbouring crop cell walls (pest resistance co-benefit). Deep rhizome MF16 creates lateral biopore network. SUPPRESSES MF13 (airflow) in interior of dense clumps — design openings for airflow management.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Areca (companion in bamboo-areca gardens), Turmeric (bamboo shade system), Ginger
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Living fence design: Bamboo clumps on farm boundary create MF11 (wind barrier) + MF1 (partial shade) + MF25 (erosion buffer) + MF17 (biomass) simultaneously. Cut bamboo every 3–4 years for maximum MF17 biomass harvest.

Validated Insight: Fastest-growing N-fixing tree in AP — established shade and N-contribution in 3–4 months. BNF rate: 60–120 kg N/ha/yr. Classic companion for paddy field bunds: Sesbania on bund provides MF23 N contribution to paddy field AND MF1 partial shade to bund edge. Leaf litter C:N ~10 — fastest decomposing high-N green manure of any AP tree.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Paddy (field bund companion), Maize (inter-canopy), Red Gram (N-fix companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Paddy bund flag: Sesbania on paddy bund is a traditional AP practice that delivers 20–30 kg N/ha to paddy field per season through leaf fall and root turnover. This practice exactly represents MF23→MF24 cascade at the field boundary.

Validated Insight: N-fixing pioneer shrub for AP degraded lands. Extremely fast establishment on poor red soils. Dense branching provides living fence + shade + N-fixation simultaneously. BNF rate: 80–120 kg N/ha/yr. Excellent for silvopasture alleys in AP's dryland zones — pollard for fodder + green manure at 60-day intervals.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Sorghum (silvopasture alley), Jowar, Cowpea (companion)
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Degraded land pioneer: Calliandra can establish on severely degraded Alfisols where Red Gram fails. Use as first-year soil builder THEN introduce Red Gram and food crops in subsequent seasons.

Validated Insight: Aromatic allelopathic shrub with strong biopesticide volatile compounds (MF26/MF21). Leaf extracts are effective against mites, aphids, and lepidoptera larvae. Used as living fence on field boundaries. Aromatic barrier effect extends 3–5m from planting. High-biomass leaf litter (MF17) decomposes moderately (C:N ~20). SUPPRESSES MF2 — does not tolerate dense shade. Traditional AP biopesticide plant.
Companion Crops (AP Recommended): Cotton (aromatic barrier), Vegetable borders, Chilli, Okra
Ontology Design Note (Rule / Flag): Biopesticide border flag: Vitex as 1–2 row border around cotton or vegetable plots provides passive aromatic pest deterrence (MF21) equivalent to 2–3 neem-based spray applications per season.
